questions.prompt: ThreatLocker's parent / child organisation hierarchy automatically propagates parent-org policies to all child organisations.
questions.explanation: There's no implicit inheritance. Propagation is via the Copy Policies operation, which is a deliberate per-target action, plus the deploy step. Treating it like inheritance and forgetting to deploy leaves children on stale policy.

questions.prompt: A custom tag at the parent organisation is referenced from a child organisation policy. What's the correct prefix format?
choices: Tag's GUID prefixed with the child organisation's name
questions.explanation: The documented format for cross-org tag references is ParentOrganization\TagName. Same pattern is used for parent-org applications; tags inside the same org use just the name.

questions.prompt: Which of these are RBAC anti-patterns at scale? (Select all that apply.)
choices: MSP staff with permanent Super Admin on every customer
Customer admins given roles at the MSP layer rather than their own org
Per-tech accounts with explicit role assignments
Shared MSP credentials used by the helpdesk team
Documented Owner role with rotation on personnel changes
questions.explanation: Permanent Super Admin everywhere, customer admins at the MSP layer, and shared credentials are the three classic anti-patterns. Per-tech accounts and documented Owner rotation are the right answers.

questions.prompt: After Copying baseline policies into a target customer org, the easy-to-forget step is:
choices: Deploying policies in the target organisation
Switching the customer to Monitor Only first
questions.explanation: The Copy Policies API explicitly notes the deploy requirement. Without it, the copied policy exists but doesn't take effect on endpoints.

questions.prompt: Maintaining old reference orgs (baseline-accounting-v3.0, v3.1, v3.2) is a heavier but more rollback-friendly version-control pattern than document-only versioning.
questions.explanation: Both patterns are valid. Reference-org-by-version is heavier (more orgs to maintain) but gives you a working rollback target. Document-only versioning is lighter but assumes the change-log is rigorously kept; the trade-off is which discipline your team will actually maintain.

questions.prompt: When customers subscribe to Cyber Hero, which responsibilities stay with the MSP? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Customer-specific judgement calls (is this contractor authorised?)
Incident triage when a request looks malicious
Quarterly customer reporting and the renewal conversation
questions.explanation: Cyber Hero clears routine transactions. Policy design, customer-specific judgement, incident triage, and the customer relationship stay with the MSP.

questions.prompt: Why use tags rather than hard-coded IP addresses in Network Control policies?
choices: Tags are addressable across the org tree, so updating a tag updates every policy that references it
Tags are mandatory for outbound policies
questions.explanation: Tags abstract destinations. When a vendor's endpoints change, you update the tag in one place and every policy referencing it picks up the change, including child orgs that reference the parent's tag.

questions.prompt: A well-designed Detect rule fires on every single matching event so nothing is missed.
questions.explanation: Single-event rules drown on-call rotations. The Occurrences condition lets you express 'fire when this happens N times in T period.' That distinguishes a one-off legitimate event from a real anomaly.

questions.prompt: A canary file at c:\users\public\financials_2026.xlsx is monitored by a Detect rule. What does it most usefully detect?
choices: Backup software running its scheduled jobs
Tooling (often malicious) that scans for finance-named files
questions.explanation: Canary files are honeypots for tooling that scans paths for finance-shaped names. A real user has no reason to touch the file; the first hit is a strong anomaly indicator.

questions.prompt: Which fields are required when defining a Network Control policy? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Destination location (IP, Tag, or for outbound only, a domain)
questions.explanation: Direction, destination, and action are always required; ports are required when allPorts is false (setting allPorts: true skips per-port specification). DNS hostnames aren't a Network Control match field; outbound destinations can be domain names but inbound rules use IP / Tag.

questions.prompt: Which artefacts make up a defensible audit pack for application control? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Coverage proof (which endpoints, which mode, since when)
Activity evidence (True denies for the period, summary plus row-level)
Change history (who modified what, when, why)
Exception register (documented divergences from the baseline with justification)
An LLM-generated narrative summarising the controls
questions.explanation: The four artefacts together form the evidence chain. Generated narratives are not evidence; if they're useful at all, they're commentary on top of the underlying CSVs.

questions.prompt: An MSP can sell ThreatLocker to a customer as 'full Essential Eight compliance in a single product.'
questions.explanation: Essential Eight covers eight controls; ThreatLocker maps cleanly to Application Control plus User Application Hardening, partly to Restrict Admin Privileges and Office Macros. The other four (patching, backups, MFA, network segmentation) need other tools. Don't over-claim.

questions.prompt: Put the ThreatLocker endpoint offboarding steps in order.
items: Export Unified Audit and System Audit evidence
Freeze policy changes for the endpoint or customer
Disable Tamper Protection through planned maintenance
Clean notifications, PSA workflow, API credentials, and stale policies
questions.explanation: Evidence comes first. Tamper Protection must be disabled before uninstall. Cleanup of routing, API, and policy artefacts happens after removal state is known.

questions.prompt: Which of these are Maintenance Modes the agent can be put into? (Select all that apply.)
questions.explanation: The Maintenance Modes are Monitor Only, Installation, Learning, Elevation, Tamper Protection Disabled, Isolation, and Lockdown. Secured is the default protected state when no Maintenance Mode is active, not a Maintenance Mode itself; treating it as one in audit evidence is a common terminology drift.
